petitioner_background: The petitioner is a doctoral candidate and researcher in the field of biomedical research, specifically working on vaccines and immunological memory (T-cell research). He sought classification as an individual of extraordinary ability in the sciences under EB-1A (INA § 203(b)(1)(A)). At the time of the fellowships discussed (2007 and 2008), he was an undergraduate student, and at the time of filing he was pursuing his doctorate.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Certificates indicating that the petitioner was twice selected for a fellowship (name redacted) and that he was selected as a fellow (name redacted) in 2007 and 2008 while an undergraduate. He also submitted documentation showing he was selected as a listee in a biographic volume (name redacted), which he equated with a prize or award.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that fellowships generally offer recipients training or educational opportunities and funding, and while they may be selective, without additional corroboration they do not qualify as prizes or awards recognizing excellence in a particular field. The petitioner provided no documentary evidence that the fellowships are nationally or internationally recognized. Fellowships are not nationally or internationally recognized prizes or awards because only other students, not established experts in the field, compete for such funding. Academic awards and honors received while preparing for a vocation generally fall short of constituting a national or international prize or award. Regarding the biographic listing, the record did not include evidence that the for-profit biographic volume is nationally or internationally recognized or that its profiles constitute prizes or awards recognizing excellence in the field.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Five articles that cite the petitioner's work.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that articles citing the petitioner's work are primarily about the authors' own work or recent trends in the field, and are not about the petitioner or his work. The regulation requires that published material be 'about the alien.' The submitted documentation did not discuss the merits of the petitioner's work, his standing in the field, any significant impact his work has had on the field, or any other information about the petitioner. The articles citing to the petitioner's work similarly referenced numerous other authors. While these research articles were relevant to the original contributions criterion, they did not meet the published material criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence that the petitioner has reviewed manuscripts submitted for publication in two journals (names redacted).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO agreed with the Director that this evidence meets the regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iv).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Letters from two professors who served on his Thesis Advisory Committee. One professor (M.D., Professor) stated the research 'offers a new potential way to enhance the effectiveness of vaccines' and is 'capable of offering a necessary breakthrough in the field of vaccinology.' Another (M.D., Chief of Department) stated the petitioner 'possesses the necessary drive and ambition to rise to the top of his field.' A letter from a scientist at an institute in India commended the petitioner's 'tremendous curiosity and enthusiasm' and noted a national journal published his research as a fellow. A letter from an Assistant Professor in Pediatrics at an institute in Thailand, his supervisor on a project, stated the petitioner's 'expertise in T-cell immunology helped expedite the project significantly.' The petitioner also provided a citation index showing one article received seven citations in 2014 and another was cited twice in 2013. On appeal he submitted a letter from a Designated Assistant Professor who cited to his work, stating the research 'is capable of making even more significant impact in the years to come' and that 'at least six different groups have reported the use of [redacted] after the publication.' In response to the RFE, a second letter expanded on joint research regarding optimal culture conditions for T-cells and claimed their joint 2014 research 'inspired many groups to start using [redacted] in culturing and expanding T-cells outside the human body.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that contributions must already be realized rather than potential or future, must be original, and must rise to the level of major significance in the field as a whole rather than to a project or organization. The letters from the thesis committee members indicated the research 'likely will have a positive impact,' but the possibility of a future impact, even profound, does not satisfy the regulatory requirements. None of the references provided examples of how the petitioner's work was already influencing the field. The citation counts (seven citations for one article in 2014, two for another in 2013) were not sufficiently explained to show a widespread impact; the petitioner did not demonstrate that a good number of researchers had been sufficiently influenced by his work. The claim that 'at least six different groups' used the technique was not corroborated by independent evidence. The AAO also noted that the petitioner's contention on appeal that certain authors 'have no history with him' directly contradicted statements in their letters, citing Matter of Ho. Solicited letters from colleagues that do not specifically identify contributions or provide specific examples of how those contributions influenced the field are insufficient. USCIS is ultimately responsible for making the final determination and may evaluate the content of expert letters. Without specific evidence showing the work has been unusually influential or widely applied, the criterion was not met.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Documentation of the petitioner's authorship of scholarly articles in professional publications (journal names redacted).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director concluded, and the AAO agreed, that the petitioner satisfied this criterion, as the record supported that finding.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal on de novo review because the petitioner established only two of the required three initial evidence criteria (judging and authorship of scholarly articles), failing to meet the lesser prizes/awards, published material, and original contributions of major significance criteria. Because the petitioner did not submit the required initial evidence, the AAO did not need to fully address a final merits determination. Nevertheless, the AAO reviewed the record in the aggregate and concluded that it did not support a finding that the petitioner had established the level of expertise required for the classification sought.
legal_citations: Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund, L.P., 51 F.3d 28 (3d Cir. 1995)
Matter of Caron International, 19 I&N Dec. 791 (Comm'r 1988)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO noted an inconsistency where the petitioner contended on appeal that two letter writers 'have no history with [the petitioner]' and served as independent experts, but this directly contradicted the claimed relationships outlined in the authors' own letters. The AAO cited Matter of Ho for the principle that an inconsistency must be resolved with independent objective evidence. No fraud finding was made.
